--- title: "Using {marginaleffects}" date: 2026-05-04 license: "CC-BY-SA 4.0" resource-path: - data - models format: html: code-link: true typst: default editor: visual fig-width: 5 fig-height: 3 knitr: opts_chunk: echo: true message: false bibliography: references.bib --- ::: {.callout-note .column-margin} ## Tutorial data [data/um_data.csv](data/um_data.csv) (4.7Mb) ::: The purpose of this tutorial is to introduce use of the `{marginaleffects}` package using linguistic data. The use of linguistic data is the distinctive aspect of this tutorial, which otherwise partially replicates the tutorials available by the author `{marginaleffects}` and collaborators [@arel-bundock2024; @arel-bundock2025; @arel-bundock2026]. I highly recommend referring to these materials as well, especially the book and reference manual at [https://marginaleffects.com](https://marginaleffects.com/). While the use of `{marginaleffects}` is often discussed within the framework of Causal Inference, it is very useful for extracting predictions and comparisons from statistical models even if you're not operating within that framework. This tutorial will use `{brms}` to fit the model, but the incredible utility of `{marginaleffects}` is its support for an enormous range of models ([123 at time of writing](https://marginaleffects.com/bonus/supported_models.html)) from a broad range of packages using a common interface. So, even if you're modeling using `{lme4}` or base `stats::lm()` or `stats::glm()`, you should be able to make use of the functions covered in this tutorial with minimal adjustment. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Setup library(tidyverse) library(brms) library(ggdist) library(marginaleffects) library(faux) library(tinytable) theme_set( theme_ggdist() + theme( palette.colour.discrete = khroma::color(palette = "bright") ) ) options( tinytable_tt_digits = 3, marginaleffects_lean = TRUE, marginaleffects_posterior_center = "mean" ) set.seed(2026) ``` ::: # Introduction: Filled Pause Data We'll be modelling the use of Filled Pauses ("um" and "uh") drawn from the Philadelphia Neighborhood Corpus (filled pause background: @clark2002; @wieling2016; @fruehwald2016). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} um = read_csv("data/um_data.csv") ``` ::: ::: {#tbl-data .cell tbl-cap='6 randomly sampled rows'} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Table code"} um |> slice_sample( by = label, n = 3 ) |> tt() |> tt_format( num_mark_big = "" ) |> tt_format( j = c(7,9:10, 12, 15), num_fmt = "decimal", digits = 2 ) |> tt_style( fontsize = 0.6 ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
speaker age gender year dob label local_speaking local_syls local_rate total_speaking total_syl total_rate prev_speaker fol_seg fol_dur
s_108 29 woman 1990 1961 UM 1.33 7 5.26 611.3 3402 5.57 0 sp 0.36
s_262 38 woman 1987 1949 UM 1.08 3 2.78 518.4 2934 5.66 0 sp 0.14
s_096 32 woman 1984 1952 UM 0.77 2 2.59 580.25 2873 4.95 0 sp 0.79
s_075 55 man 2010 1955 UH 1 9 9 1042.85 5987 5.74 0 AH1 0.03
s_095 41 man 1974 1933 UH 1.18 7 5.93 932.77 5086 5.45 0 sp 0.19
s_078 57 man 1985 1928 UH 1.29 6 4.65 737.95 3543 4.8 0 AH0 0.06
``` ::: ::: Every row corresponds to either an "um" or an "un". The data columns that may not be obvious from the names are: - `local_*`: quantities estimated based on a 1 second window around each filled pause - `*_speaking`: total duration of words spoken - `*_syls`: The number of syllables spoken - `*_rate`: The number of syllables divided by the speaking time. - `total_*`: Quantities estimated based on the speaker's entire interview. - `*_speaking`, `*_syls`, `*_rate`: same as for the local quantities - `prev_speaker`: If 0, the previous speaker in the recording was the the same as the focal speaker. If any other number, it was a different speaker. - `fol_seg`: The phone following the filled pause. `sp` is a pause, and every other value is a CMU-style label. - `fol_dur`: The duration of the following phone. ## Descriptive stats @clark2002 argue that filled pauses are useful signals to a listener that a speaker is working harder for lexical access, and that "um" is a signal of greater effort than "um". As such, other indicators of greater effort in lexical access should be predictive of "um" usage, such as whether or not there is a silent pause following, and slowdowns in local speech rate. @fig-rate plots "um" usage against the relative speech rate (how much faster/slower the local speech rate around the filled pause is relative to the speaker's overall speech rate) and whether or not the following segment was a silent pause. It appears to hold true that a following silent pause is predictive of higher "um" usage, and so is a slower relative speech rate. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Plotting code"} um |> mutate( um = (label == "UM") * 1, fol_pause = fol_seg == "sp" ) |> ggplot( aes(local_rate/total_rate, um, color = fol_pause) ) + geom_dots( aes( side = label, order = fol_pause ), height = 0.5, group = NA, alpha = 0.1 ) + stat_smooth( method = glm, method.args = list( family = binomial ) ) + scale_side_mirrored( guide = "none" ) + scale_x_log10() + scale_y_continuous( expand = expansion(0) ) + coord_cartesian( y = c(0,1) ) + theme_sub_legend( position = "inside", position.inside = c(0.9,0.5) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![The relationship between relative speech rate and silent pause on "um" usage.](index_files/figure-html/fig-rate-1.png){#fig-rate width=480} ::: ::: @wieling2016 and @fruehwald2016 also found that there appears to be a language change in progress, with speakers shifting towards higher "um" usage. As with most language changes in progress, women are in the lead. @fig-gender plots these relationships in the data. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Plotting code"} um |> mutate( um = (label == "UM") * 1, ) |> ggplot( aes(dob, um, color = gender) ) + geom_dots( aes( side = label, order = gender ), height = 0.5, group = NA, alpha = 0.1 ) + stat_smooth( method = glm, method.args = list( family = binomial ) ) + scale_side_mirrored( guide = "none" ) + scale_x_log10() + scale_y_continuous( expand = expansion(0) ) + coord_cartesian( y = c(0,1) ) + theme_sub_legend( position = "inside", position.inside = c(0.9,0.2) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![The relationship between date of birth and gender on "um" usage.](index_files/figure-html/fig-gender-1.png){#fig-gender width=480} ::: ::: ## Preparing the data For modeling, I'll fit a logistic regression predicting "um" (vs "uh") usage. I'll calculate some derived predictors, specifically the relative speech rate, and categorical predictor encoding whether the following sound was a silent pause, a vowel, or a consonant. I'll also use sum contrasts for all of the predictors. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} um |> mutate( um = (label == "UM")*1, relative_rate = local_rate/total_rate, fol_class = case_when( fol_seg == "sp" ~ "pause", str_detect(fol_seg, "[AEIOU]") ~ "vowel", .default = "consonant" ) |> contr_code_sum(), gender = contr_code_sum(gender) ) -> to_mod ``` ::: To aid with model fitting, I'll need some summary statistics for the between-speaker variables. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} um |> slice( .by = speaker, 1 ) |> summarise( across( c(dob, starts_with("total")), mean ) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 4 dob total_speaking total_syl total_rate 1 1943. 861. 4558. 5.30 ``` ::: ::: # Fitting the model For fitting the model, I want to take into account the fact that - "um" usage is shifting over DOB, possibly at different rates for men and women. - The rate of speech and following pause effects may change as the overall rate of "um" usage increases. - Speakers who are already fast speakers might not have as strong a relative speech rate effect. Taking all of these things into account results in this monstrous model formula. I'm centering and log transforming the predictors inside the model formula, because this will eventually play nicer with the `{marginaleffects}` functions. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} model_formula <- bf( um ~ I(dob-1943) * gender * fol_class * I(log(total_rate/5.3)) * I(log(relative_rate)) + (log2(relative_rate) + fol_class | speaker) ) ``` ::: In case you missed it, that's a **five way** interaction. Now we'll fit it using brms and default priors. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} um_model <- brm( model_formula, family = bernoulli(), data = to_mod, backend = "cmdstanr", cores = 4, file = "models/um_model" ) ``` ::: This model results in *48* fixed effects estimates, and because they're all involved in interactions, none of them can be straightforwardly interpreted. ::: {#tbl-fixef .cell tbl-cap='Fixed effects estimates '} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Table code"} fixef(um_model) |> as_tibble(rownames = "term") -> fixef_df reliable <- function(lo, hi){ sign(lo) == sign(hi) } fixef_df |> mutate( dob = case_when( str_detect(term, "dob") ~ "dob" ), gender = case_when( str_detect(term, "gender") ~ "man" ), fol_class = case_when( str_detect(term, "fol_class") ~ str_extract( term, "fol_class.([a-z]+)M", group = 1 ) ), total_rate = case_when( str_detect(term, "total_rate") ~ "total_rate" ), rel_rate = case_when( str_detect(term, "relative_rate") ~ "rel_rate" ) ) |> relocate( dob:last_col(), .before = term ) |> select(-term) |> slice(-1) -> param_df param_df |> tt() |> tt_format( replace = TRUE ) |> tt_format( j = 6:9, digits = 3, num_fmt = "decimal" ) |> tt_style( i = 1:nrow(param_df), bold = reliable( param_df$Q2.5, param_df$Q97.5 ) ) |> theme_html( engine = "tabulator", tabulator_pagination = c(10, 25, 50) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html} tinytable_e95zhvfhzti5s053t1d9

``` ::: ::: # From model to meaning ## Getting predictions We can get the probability of "um" the model assigns to each token with `marginaleffects::predictions()`. Without passing any other arguments, it will give us back predictions for the original data. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} predictions( um_model ) -> all_pred ``` ::: ::: {#tbl-pred .cell tbl-cap='Sampled rows from the item level predictions. '} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Table code"} all_pred|> slice_sample( by = label, n = 3 ) |> tt() |> tt_style( fontsize = 0.6 ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
rowid estimate conf.low conf.high speaker age gender year dob label local_speaking local_syls local_rate total_speaking total_syl total_rate prev_speaker fol_seg fol_dur um relative_rate fol_class df
16919 0.6723 0.4721 0.8404 s_010 65 man 2006 1941 UM 0.488 1 2.05 1705 8600 5.04 3 sp 0.08 1 0.406 pause Inf
22759 0.8658 0.761 0.9452 s_194 18 man 2000 1982 UM 1.021 5 4.9 1615 9887 6.12 0 sp 0.169 1 0.8 pause Inf
22010 0.3568 0.1957 0.5435 s_072 45 man 1997 1952 UM 1.06 5 4.72 718 3919 5.46 0 sp 0.32 1 0.864 pause Inf
14210 0.0166 0.0012 0.0598 s_058 69 woman 1982 1913 UH 1.32 5 3.79 881 4592 5.21 0 sp 0.09 0 0.727 pause Inf
27858 0.2748 0.1149 0.4785 s_304 65 woman 2006 1941 UH 0.648 2 3.09 2170 12956 5.97 0 sp 0.19 0 0.517 pause Inf
9852 0.0912 0.0445 0.1546 s_044 24 man 1996 1972 UH 0.641 2 3.12 1333 8411 6.31 0 sp 0.23 0 0.494 pause Inf
``` ::: ::: ### Average predictions One thing we could ask is what the average prediction for, say, the `fol_class` variable is. To get that, we can do some tidyverse summarizing on `all_pred`. This is also called "marginalizing" over all of the other predictors. ::: {#tbl-avg1 .cell tbl-cap='Predictions averaged over `fol_class`'} ```{.r .cell-code} all_pred |> summarise( .by = fol_class, estimate = mean(estimate) ) |> arrange(desc(estimate)) |> tt() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
fol_class estimate
pause 0.2722
vowel 0.103
consonant 0.0922
``` ::: ::: The `marginaleffects::avg_predictions()` does this kind of marginalization in one step. ::: {#tbl-avg2 .cell tbl-cap='Predictions averaged over `fol_class`'} ```{.r .cell-code} avg_predictions( um_model, by = "fol_class" ) |> arrange(desc(estimate)) |> tt() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
fol_class estimate conf.low conf.high df
pause 0.2722 0.2679 0.2766 Inf
vowel 0.103 0.0936 0.1124 Inf
consonant 0.0922 0.0865 0.0978 Inf
``` ::: ::: ### Prediction type By default, predictions (and average predictions) are made on the "response" scale. For this logistic regression, that means these are probabilities. Here's the same average probability by gender. ::: {#tbl-avg-gender .cell tbl-cap='Predictions averaged over `gender`'} ```{.r .cell-code} avg_predictions( um_model, by = "gender" ) |> tt() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
gender estimate conf.low conf.high df
man 0.144 0.14 0.148 Inf
woman 0.299 0.294 0.305 Inf
``` ::: ::: For some purposes, we'll want to work with predictions on the "link" scale, which for logistic regression will be in log-odds. ::: {#tbl-avg-logit .cell tbl-cap='Predictions averaged over `gender` in logit space.'} ```{.r .cell-code} avg_predictions( um_model, by = "gender", type = "link" ) -> gender_logit gender_logit |> tt() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
gender estimate conf.low conf.high df
man -3.39 -3.56 -3.24 Inf
woman -1.65 -1.76 -1.55 Inf
``` ::: ::: Due to the nature of probability space vs logit space, applying the inverse logit to predictions won't be identical. ::: {#tbl-avg-invlogit .cell tbl-cap='Inverse logit of the average logit predictions by `gender`'} ```{.r .cell-code} gender_logit |> mutate( across( where(is.numeric), plogis ) ) |> tt() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
gender estimate conf.low conf.high df
man 0.0325 0.0276 0.0378 1
woman 0.1607 0.1466 0.1752 1
``` ::: ::: A lot of the appeal of using `{marginaleffects}` is getting predictions and comparisons on the original scale of the data. However, since this filled-pause data has predicted probabilities ranging from so close to 0 up to the middle of the probability space, it's advisable to carry out the remaining analyses in the logit space, or at least do both and compare the results. ## Getting Treatment Effects We can estimate the average effect of gender, keeping all other factors the same, by calculating the Average Treatment Effect (ATE). To do this, we create two copies of the original dataset: one where all `gender` has been set to `woman`, the other where it's all set to `man`. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} to_mod |> mutate( gender = "woman" ) -> faux_woman to_mod |> mutate( gender = "man" ) -> faux_man ``` ::: Then, we can get the predicted values for these two counterfactual datasets. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} predictions( um_model, newdata = faux_woman, type = "link" ) -> faux_woman_pred predictions( um_model, newdata = faux_man, type = "link" ) -> faux_man_pred ``` ::: Then, if we take the difference between the predictions and average them, we'll get the ATE. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} mean( faux_woman_pred$estimate - faux_man_pred$estimate ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 1.703695 ``` ::: ::: A way to do this more directly is to use `marginaleffects::avg_comparisons()`. ::: {#tbl-gender-comp .cell tbl-cap='Average Treatment Effect for gender'} ```{.r .cell-code} avg_comparisons( um_model, variables = "gender", type = "link" ) |> tt() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
term contrast estimate conf.low conf.high
gender woman - man 1.7 1.27 2.14
``` ::: ::: ### Thinking about these comparisons Just to make it crystal clear what's happening with these average comparisons, let's grab one row from the original data. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} to_mod |> slice(26012) |> select(speaker, dob, gender, total_rate, relative_rate, fol_class) -> one_row one_row |> tt() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
speaker dob gender total_rate relative_rate fol_class
s_114 1927 woman 4.92 0.592 pause
``` ::: ::: When we get the predictions for this row, it's taking into account all of the parameters of the model (including the random effects!) to give us the predicted outcome. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} predictions( um_model, newdata = one_row, type = "link" ) |> as_tibble() -> orig_pred orig_pred |> tt() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
rowid estimate conf.low conf.high speaker dob gender total_rate relative_rate fol_class df
1 -0.115 -0.9 0.693 s_114 1927 woman 4.92 0.592 pause Inf
``` ::: ::: Then, we get a counterfactual prediction grid, keeping all of the values the same except `gender`, which we swap. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} one_row |> mutate( gender = "man" ) -> counterfactual_row ``` ::: When we get the predicted value for *this* row, it'll be like asking "What is the predicted outcome if we kept all of the other influences of speech rate, dob, following segment, and random effects the same, but now `gender` is 'man'?" ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} predictions( um_model, newdata = counterfactual_row, type = "link" ) |> as_tibble() -> counter_pred ``` ::: We can compare these two unit-level predictions. ::: {#tbl-unit-comp .cell tbl-cap='Comparison of unit level predictions when changing the value of `gender` '} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Table code"} avg_comparisons( um_model, variable = "gender", newdata = one_row, type = "link" ) |> as_tibble() |> mutate( type = "diff" ) |> select(-gender) |> rename( gender = contrast ) -> ex_comp bind_rows( orig_pred, counter_pred, ) |> mutate( type = "pred" ) |> bind_rows( ex_comp ) |> select( colnames(orig_pred), type, -rowid, -df ) |> relocate( gender, .after = conf.high ) |> relocate( type, .before = 1 ) |> tt() |> tt_style( i = 2, line = "b" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
type estimate conf.low conf.high gender speaker dob total_rate relative_rate fol_class
pred -0.115 -0.9 0.693 woman s_114 1927 4.92 0.592 pause
pred -2.476 -3.53 -1.431 man s_114 1927 4.92 0.592 pause
diff 2.361 1.64 3.072 woman - man s_114 1927 4.92 0.592 pause
``` ::: ::: For just this one row of data, changing `gender="man"` to `gender="woman"` (while keeping everything else the same) shifts the predicted outcome by about 2.4 logits. ::: {.callout-note collapse="true"} ## Aside `speaker` is an important grouping variable in the data, and `gender` is a between-group predictor. When estimating the average treatment effect of a between-group variable, we might want to use a summarized version of the original data. This code will give is the first row from each speaker for each following segment, with a fixed `relative_rate` of 1. The `total_rate` predictor is another between-group variable. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} to_mod |> slice( .by = c(speaker, fol_class), 1 ) |> mutate( relative_rate = 1 ) -> speaker_grid ``` ::: Now we can get the average treatment effect based on this speaker grid. I'll further break out the effect by `fol_class`, because not every level of `fol_class` was equally frequent, so collapsing across it in this speaker grid may give us an unrepresentative estimate. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} avg_comparisons( um_model, variables = "gender", by = "fol_class", newdata = speaker_grid, type = "link" ) |> arrange(desc(estimate)) |> tt() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
term contrast fol_class estimate conf.low conf.high
gender woman - man pause 1.64 1.229 2.05
gender woman - man vowel 1.56 0.626 2.55
gender woman - man consonant 1.11 0.514 1.7
``` ::: ::: ::: ### More kinds of comparisons Looking back at the average predictions across `fol_class`, it looked like if there was a following vowel, "um" was slightly more likely than a consonant. We can double check if this is the case by calculating all pairwise comparisons of levels in `fol_class`. ::: {#tbl-fol-comp .cell tbl-cap='Average Treatment Effect for following class'} ```{.r .cell-code} avg_comparisons( um_model, variables = list( fol_class = "pairwise" ), type = "link" ) |> tt() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
term contrast estimate conf.low conf.high
fol_class pause - consonant 1.786 1.52 2.061
fol_class vowel - consonant -0.355 -1.06 0.229
fol_class vowel - pause -2.141 -2.81 -1.584
``` ::: ::: There are several kinds of comparisons we can make for both categorical and numeric data. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} avg_comparisons( um_model, variables = list( # comparison to a reference level gender = "reference", # pairwise comparison of all levels fol_class = "pairwise", # effect of adding +20 to dob dob = 20, # comparing two specific reference values relative_rate = c(2/1, 1/2), # comparing the 3rd and 1st quartile total_rate = "iqr" ), type = "link" ) -> all_comp ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ::: ::: {#tbl-all-ate .cell tbl-cap='Average Marginal Effects '} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Table code"} all_comp |> tt() |> tt_group(i = term) |> subset(select = -term) |> style_tt("~groupi", j = 1, indent = 2, align = 'r') |> style_tt("groupi", align = "l") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
contrast estimate conf.low conf.high
dob dob dob dob
+20 1.33 1.153 1.50742
fol_class fol_class fol_class fol_class
pause - consonant 1.786 1.522 2.06062
vowel - consonant -0.355 -1.064 0.22859
vowel - pause -2.141 -2.813 -1.58391
gender gender gender gender
woman - man 1.704 1.267 2.13962
relative_rate relative_rate relative_rate relative_rate
2 - 0.5 -2.11 -2.428 -1.78701
total_rate total_rate total_rate total_rate
Q3 - Q1 -0.237 -0.477 0.00994
``` ::: ::: @tbl-all-ate doesn't tell the whole story of what's going on in a complex model like this, but it could be a useful (and more legible) format for reporting model effects. ## Using prediction grids Instead of using the entire original dataset, we might want to get (average) predicted outcomes at *specific* values across predictors. That's where `marginaleffects::datagrid()` comes in. Passing our model to `datagrid()` gives us back a 1 row data frame with a column for every predictor in the model, and the values are either the average or most common value of that predictor. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} datagrid( model = um_model ) |> tt() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
rowid dob fol_class gender relative_rate speaker total_rate um
1 1939 pause man 0.911 s_036 5.29 0
``` ::: ::: We can manually set some values in the datagrid by passing them as arguments. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} datagrid( model = um_model, relative_rate = 1, gender = c("man", "woman") ) |> tt() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
rowid dob fol_class speaker total_rate um relative_rate gender
1 1939 pause s_036 5.29 0 1 man
2 1939 pause s_036 5.29 0 1 woman
``` ::: ::: We can also provide a function that when applied to the original data columns returns values for the datagrid. For categorical variables `unique` is a good option. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} datagrid( model = um_model, fol_class = unique ) |> tt() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
rowid dob gender relative_rate speaker total_rate um fol_class
1 1939 man 0.911 s_036 5.29 0 consonant
2 1939 man 0.911 s_036 5.29 0 pause
3 1939 man 0.911 s_036 5.29 0 vowel
``` ::: ::: Here's a parameterized function to return `n` evenly spaced quantiles for a continuous variable. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} n_quantiles <- function(n){ function(x){ quantile(x, probs = ppoints(n)) } } ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} datagrid( model = um_model, total_rate = n_quantiles(3) ) |> tt() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
rowid dob fol_class gender relative_rate speaker um total_rate
1 1939 pause man 0.911 s_036 0 4.87
2 1939 pause man 0.911 s_036 0 5.24
3 1939 pause man 0.911 s_036 0 5.71
``` ::: ::: Using prediction grids this way is what I would recommend for getting most prediction and slope estimates, especially for non-linear models. ## Getting expected predictions and slopes We can get the "expected" predictions from the model by setting up a representative prediction grid. We'll start with a focus on the language change in progress. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} dob_gender_grid <- datagrid( model = um_model, gender = unique, dob = 1900:1990, total_rate = 5.3, relative_rate = 1, fol_class = "pause" ) ``` ::: When we use this prediction grid, we'll want to set `re_formula = NA`. This is a brms specific argument that sets the random effects to 0 when getting our predictions. We want to do this because the `speaker` variable is set to the value of the speaker with the most data. They're not necessarily a representative speaker, so we don't want to include the random intercepts and slopes when estimating the predictions. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} um_model |> predictions( newdata = dob_gender_grid, re_formula = NA ) -> dob_gender_pred ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Plotting code"} dob_gender_pred |> ggplot( aes(dob, estimate, color = gender) ) + geom_lineribbon( aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high), alpha = 0.8 ) + ylim(0,1) + theme_sub_legend( position = "inside", position.inside = c(0.2, 0.5) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Estimated "um" probability by date of birth and gender](index_files/figure-html/fig-change-1.png){#fig-change width=480} ::: ::: One of the questions we had was whether the rate of change was the same for men and women. We can get estimated slopes over year of birth with the `slopes()` and `avg_slopes()` functions. This next table is telling us what the average effect of 1 year of dob is on the log-odds of "um". ::: {#tbl-slope1 .cell tbl-cap='Average expected slope over date of birth'} ```{.r .cell-code} avg_slopes( um_model, variables = "dob", newdata = dob_gender_grid, type = "link", re_formula = NA ) |> tt() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
term contrast estimate conf.low conf.high
dob dY/dX 0.0678 0.0579 0.0778
``` ::: ::: We can also break this out by gender. ::: {#tbl-slope2 .cell tbl-cap='Average expected slope over date of birth, by gender'} ```{.r .cell-code} avg_slopes( um_model, variables = "dob", by = "gender", newdata = dob_gender_grid, type = "link", re_formula = NA ) -> dob_by_gender dob_by_gender |> tt() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
term contrast gender estimate conf.low conf.high
dob dY/dX man 0.0763 0.061 0.0916
dob dY/dX woman 0.0593 0.0466 0.0718
``` ::: ::: The estimated slope for men is slightly steeper than for women. Another way to explore this is to look at how much the difference between men and women has changed over dob. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} avg_slopes( um_model, variables = "gender", by = "dob", newdata = dob_gender_grid, type = "link", re_formula = NA ) -> gender_by_dob ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Plotting code."} gender_by_dob |> ggplot( aes(dob, estimate) ) + geom_hline( yintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.5 ) + geom_lineribbon( aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high), alpha = 0.8 ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Estimated gender effect over DOB ](index_files/figure-html/fig-gender-slope-1.png){#fig-gender-slope width=480} ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note collapse="true"} ## Aside It's not straightforward from looking at just the estimated slopes by gender in @tbl-slope1 to tell whether the slopes are reliably different. However, because we fit the model with brms, we can extract the posterior draws from the marginal effects object, and carry out some additional estimation. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} dob_by_gender |> posterior_draws() |> arrange(gender) |> summarise( .by = drawid, interaction = diff(draw) ) |> mean_hdci(interaction) |> tt() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
interaction .lower .upper .width .point .interval
-0.017 -0.0368 0.00329 0.95 mean hdci
``` ::: ::: The interaction effect doesn't seem that reliable. ::: Now, let's turn to speech rate effects. At a first pass, we'll get just the predicted log-odds of "um" over relative speech rate. For this, I'll set up a relatively balanced prediction grid across all predictors. I'm making the spread of values across the numeric predictors a little less dense to speed up computation. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} rate_grid <- datagrid( model = um_model, relative_rate = seq(1/3, 3/1, length = 10), total_rate = n_quantiles(10), gender = unique, fol_class = unique, dob = seq(1900, 1990, length = 10) ) ``` ::: Now, I'll get the average predicted "um" probability across `relative_rate`. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} avg_predictions( um_model, newdata = rate_grid, by = c("relative_rate"), re_formula = NA ) -> rate_pred ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Plotting code"} rate_pred |> ggplot( aes(relative_rate, estimate) ) + geom_lineribbon( aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high) ) + scale_x_log10() + ylim(0,1) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Expected 'um' probability by `relative_rate`, marginalizing over all other predictors ](index_files/figure-html/fig-rate-pred-1.png){#fig-rate-pred width=480} ::: ::: We can break down the these predictions by another hesitation indicator: the following segment ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} avg_predictions( um_model, newdata = rate_grid, by = c("relative_rate", "fol_class"), re_formula = NA ) -> rate_by_fol ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Plotting code"} rate_by_fol |> ggplot( aes(relative_rate, estimate, color = fol_class) ) + geom_lineribbon( aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high), alpha = 0.6 ) + geom_line( linewidth = 1.5 ) + ylim(0, 1) + scale_x_log10() + theme_sub_legend( position = "inside", position.inside = c(0.85, 0.5) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Expected 'um' probability by `relative_rate`, and `fol_class`, marginalizing over all other predictors ](index_files/figure-html/fig-rate-fol-pred-1.png){#fig-rate-fol-pred width=480} ::: ::: One of the questions I'd asked above is whether the effect of relative speech rate is affected by how baseline fast or slow a speaker is. We can explore that here with `avg_slopes()`. Because `relative_rate` is a ratio, I'll set `slope = "dyex"`. This means the estimated slopes will describe how the log-odds of "um" changes as `relative_rate` increases by 100% (instead of +1). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} avg_slopes( um_model, newdata = rate_grid, variables = "relative_rate", by = "total_rate", slope = "dyex", type = "link", re_formula = NA ) -> rate_by_total_slope ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Plotting code."} rate_by_total_slope |> ggplot( aes(total_rate, estimate) ) + geom_lineribbon( aes( ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high ) ) + scale_x_log10() + labs( y = "relative rate slope" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Estimated slope of `relative_rate` by `total_rate` ](index_files/figure-html/fig-rate-total-1.png){#fig-rate-total width=480} ::: ::: It doesn't seem like someone being an overall slower or faster talker interacts with the effect of relative speech rate. Another thing to ask is whether the effect of relative speech rate has been affected by the language change! To look at this, we'll get the average slope of `relative_rate`, grouped by date of birth and gender. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} avg_slopes( um_model, newdata = rate_grid, variables = "relative_rate", by = c("dob", "gender"), slope = "dyex", type = "link", re_formula = NA ) -> rate_by_change_slope ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Plotting code."} rate_by_change_slope |> ggplot( aes(dob, estimate, color = gender) ) + geom_hline( yintercept = 0 ) + geom_lineribbon( aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high), alpha = 0.6 ) + geom_line( linewidth = 1.5 ) + theme_sub_legend( position = "inside", position.inside = c(0.5, 0.9), direction = "horizontal" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Estimated slope of `relative_rate` by `dob` and `gender` ](index_files/figure-html/fig-rate-change-1.png){#fig-rate-change width=480} ::: ::: The result is a bit unexpected: It looks like the effect of relative speech rate has been growing stronger for women, and was never all that reliable for men. # More kinds of models As I said above, while I used `{brms}` to fit the model for this tutorial, the functions covered here would work in just the same way for many other kinds of models. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} flat_um_model <- glm( um ~ I(dob-1943) * gender * fol_class * I(log(total_rate/5.3)) * I(log(relative_rate)), data = to_mod, family = binomial ) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} flat_um_model |> predictions( newdata = datagrid( dob = 1900:1990, gender = unique, fol_class = "pause", relative_rate = 1 ) ) |> ggplot( aes(dob, estimate, color = gender) ) + geom_lineribbon( aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high), alpha = 0.8 )+ ylim(0,1) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-34-1.png){width=480} ::: ::: They also work well for non-linear smooths. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} non_linear <- brm( um ~ gender + s(I(dob-1943), by = gender) + (1|speaker), data = to_mod, family = bernoulli(), backend = "cmdstanr", cores = 4, file = "models/non_linear" ) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} non_linear |> predictions( newdata = datagrid( dob = 1900:1990, gender = unique ), re_formula = NA ) |> ggplot( aes(dob, estimate, color = gender) ) + geom_lineribbon( aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high), alpha = 0.8 )+ ylim(0,1) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-36-1.png){width=480} ::: ::: We can even get a difference curve ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} non_linear |> slopes( newdata = datagrid( dob = 1900:1990, gender = unique ), variables = "gender", by = "dob", type = "link", re_formula = NA ) |> ggplot( aes(dob, estimate) ) + geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.5)+ geom_lineribbon( aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high), alpha = 0.8 ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-37-1.png){width=480} ::: ::: # Further reading I can't cover everything worth knowing about the marginaleffects package in a single tutorial. For further reading I would recommend: - [The marginal effects webpage](https://marginaleffects.com/) [@arel-bundock2025] - These blog posts by Andrew Heiss: - [Marginalia](https://www.andrewheiss.com/blog/2022/05/20/marginalia/) [@heiss] - [Marginal and conditional effects for GLMMs with {marginaleffects}](https://www.andrewheiss.com/blog/2022/11/29/conditional-marginal-marginaleffects/) [@heiss2022]
